content.assessment: This is the only proposal that squarely requires the highest-order legal authority change identified in the evidence base: because the 6- and 8-year gimnázium tracks are codified in primary legislation (Act CXC of 2011), delaying first tracking to age 14+ necessitates parliamentary amendment, not administrative or ministerial action (F-live-legal_and_governance-01). This raises the legal-authority bar substantially relative to t1 and t2. Bundling structural delay with catchment/anti-sorting rules to address primary-school-choice segregation directly confronts the constitutionally protected free-choice guarantee (F-live-legal_and_governance-02), creating a foreseeable and significant rights-compatibility conflict: restricting choice-driven sorting is not straightforward under current constitutional doctrine. Further, the proposal's own logic acknowledges (via F-live-legal_and_governance-08) that tracking-age reform alone has limited systemic effect without addressing the other three selection points, meaning full realization of stated goals would require yet further legal/administrative changes beyond the core statutory amendment. Jurisdictional fragmentation (F-live-legal_and_governance-07) also threatens enforceability: church, foundation, and private schools sit outside Klebelsberg Centre's authority, so even a successful parliamentary reform of the state system could leave a parallel non-state selective channel intact, undermining the reform's structural intent and creating an accountability gap between legislative intent and effective coverage. This governance tension is itself flagged directly by the evidence (F-live-legal_and_governance-10): unresolved authority-locus ambiguity among Parliament, Klebelsberg Centre, individual maintainers, and church/private entities would need explicit resolution as part of implementation, not as an afterthought.
opportunities: A parliamentary process, while slower, could provide more durable legal entrenchment against reversal compared to ministerial-discretion-based approaches used in other proposals

Explicit legislative reform creates an opportunity to simultaneously clarify the fragmented four-point authority structure identified in the evidence (F-live-legal_and_governance-08, F-live-legal_and_governance-10)
strengths: Directly and appropriately matches proposal ambition to the required legal instrument: structural change requires parliamentary legislation, and the proposal is framed at that scale (F-live-legal_and_governance-01)
Bundling with catchment/anti-sorting rules shows awareness of the multi-point selection structure and attempts a more systemically coherent governance design (F-live-legal_and_governance-08)
threats: Church/foundation/private schools outside Klebelsberg jurisdiction may resist inclusion or be exempted from the reform, preserving a segregated selective channel outside state control and undermining the policy's structural intent (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
Constitutional challenge to catchment/anti-sorting provisions is a substantial and foreseeable threat to enforceability given the free-choice guarantee (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
Unresolved governance-authority-locus question could stall implementation even after legislative passage (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)
weaknesses: Requires parliamentary amendment to primary legislation, a substantially higher legal-authority bar and slower, more contested process than administrative alternatives (F-live-legal_and_governance-01)

Catchment/anti-sorting rules bundled with structural delay directly implicate the constitutional free-choice guarantee, creating a foreseeable rights-compatibility conflict without an evident resolution mechanism in the proposal (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)

Authority-locus ambiguity (Parliament vs. Klebelsberg Centre vs. maintainers vs. church/private entities) is unresolved and would require explicit clarification as part of any implementation (F-live-legal_and_governance-10)
